name: Kabsa (Saudi Spiced Chicken Rice)
description: The national dish of Saudi Arabia — fragrant long-grain rice cooked in a rich chicken broth perfumed with dried limes, cinnamon, and a bouquet of Gulf spices.
instructions: Heat ghee in a large pot; brown chicken pieces on all sides. Remove and set aside.
In the same pot, fry onions until deep golden. Add tomatoes and tomato paste; cook 5 minutes.
Add whole spices, dried limes, and kabsa spice mix. Stir 1 minute.
Return chicken; pour in 4 cups water. Season, bring to boil, then simmer covered 30 minutes.
Remove chicken and strain the broth. Measure 4.5 cups of broth.
Return broth to pot; bring to boil. Add drained rice. Bring back to boil, then cover tightly on low heat 20 minutes.
Rest rice off heat 5 minutes. Fluff and plate. Top with chicken pieces, raisins, and toasted almonds.
tips: Dried limes (loomi) are the signature flavour of Gulf cuisine — find them in Middle Eastern grocers. Pierce them before use.

name: Maqluba (Upside-Down Lamb and Rice)
description: Dramatic and theatrical, Maqluba (Arabic for 'upside-down') is a Palestinian and Jordanian showpiece of layered lamb, fried vegetables, and fragrant rice, flipped at the table.
instructions: Simmer lamb with onion, cinnamon stick, allspice, and salt in 6 cups water until tender, about 50 minutes. Reserve broth.
Salt eggplant slices, rest 20 minutes, dry thoroughly. Fry until golden; also fry cauliflower.
Layer the pot: start with tomato slices on the bottom, then fried vegetables, then lamb.
Mix rice with ground spices. Layer rice over the lamb, pressing down gently.
Pour 4.5 cups of the reserved lamb broth over the rice. Bring to boil over medium heat.
Cover tightly and cook on low 30 minutes until rice absorbs all liquid. Rest 10 minutes.
Place a large serving platter over the pot; flip in one confident motion. Lift the pot slowly. Garnish with pine nuts and parsley.
tips: The key to a perfect flip: let the rice rest off-heat and don't rush. Line the pot bottom with tomatoes to prevent sticking.

name: Chicken Shawarma
description: The Middle East's greatest street food — marinated chicken thighs stacked on a vertical spit (or oven-roasted), wrapped in warm bread with garlic sauce, pickles, and tomatoes.

ingredient_quantities.garlic sauce: toum or garlic mayo, for serving

instructions: Combine yogurt, lemon juice, garlic, all spices, oil, and salt. Coat chicken; marinate at least 2 hours.
Grill chicken thighs on high heat, 5–6 minutes per side until charred and cooked through. Or roast at 220°C for 30 minutes.
Rest meat 5 minutes, then slice thin against the grain.
Warm pita briefly on a dry pan.
Spread garlic sauce on pita. Layer chicken slices, tomatoes, onion, and pickles.
Roll tightly in foil to hold, cut in half, and serve immediately.
tips: Don't skip the overnight marination — it makes the chicken incomparably juicy. The garlic sauce (toum) is the secret weapon.

name: Mutabbal (Smoky Eggplant Dip)
description: Silkier and richer than baba ganoush, Mutabbal blends fire-roasted eggplant with tahini, lemon, and garlic into a smoky, creamy dip drizzled with olive oil and pomegranate seeds.
instructions: Char the eggplants directly over a gas flame or under a broiler, turning often, until the skin is completely blackened and the inside is collapsed — 15–20 minutes.
Place in a colander, slit open, and let cool and drain for 10 minutes.
Scoop out the flesh, discarding all charred skin. Chop roughly.
Mix eggplant flesh with tahini, lemon juice, garlic, cumin, and salt.
Drizzle with olive oil, scatter pomegranate seeds and parsley. Serve with warm pita.
tips: Maximum char on the outside = maximum smokiness inside. Don't rush the charring stage. Drain well so dip isn't watery.

name: Knafeh (Cheese Pastry in Sugar Syrup)
description: Lebanon's most beloved dessert — gossamer-thin shredded pastry filled with gooey stretchy cheese, soaked in orange blossom syrup, and crowned with crushed pistachios.
instructions: Desalt akkawi: soak in cold water 2 hours, changing water every 30 minutes. Shred both cheeses and mix.
Make syrup: dissolve sugar in water, boil 5 minutes with lemon juice. Off heat, add orange blossom water and rose water. Cool.
Melt clarified butter. Pull kataifi apart into strands; coat thoroughly with butter.
Press half the kataifi into a greased round 30cm pan as the base.
Spread the cheese filling evenly over the kataifi base.
Top with remaining kataifi; press firmly. Bake at 190°C for 25–30 minutes until deep golden.
Flip immediately onto a plate. Pour cold syrup over hot knafeh. Garnish with pistachios. Serve at once.
tips: The contrast of hot knafeh and cold syrup is what makes it magical — don't let the syrup warm before pouring.

name: Shish Tawook (Grilled Chicken Skewers)
description: Beloved Lebanese grilled chicken cubes marinated in a tangy yogurt-lemon-tomato mixture, perfectly charred on skewers and served with toum garlic sauce and warm bread.
instructions: Mix yogurt, tomato paste, lemon juice, garlic, oil, and all spices. Toss chicken cubes in marinade; refrigerate at least 2 hours.
Thread chicken alternating with bell pepper and onion cubes onto metal skewers.
Grill on a hot BBQ or griddle pan, turning every 2–3 minutes until charred and cooked through, about 12 minutes.
Serve with toum garlic sauce, sliced tomatoes, and pita bread.
tips: Don't cut chicken too small — 4cm cubes stay juicy. Soak wooden skewers 30 minutes before using.

name: Ful Medames (Spiced Fava Bean Stew)
description: Egypt's national breakfast — a hearty, sustaining stew of slow-cooked fava beans mashed with olive oil, garlic, lemon, and cumin, eaten with hard-boiled eggs and warm bread.
instructions: Place fava beans in a pot with 1 cup water. Heat on medium; mash about 1/3 of the beans roughly with a fork.
Add garlic, cumin, and chilli flakes. Simmer 5 minutes.
Stir in lemon juice, olive oil, and salt. Cook 2 more minutes.
Spoon into a bowl; top with diced tomato, parsley, and a generous drizzle of olive oil.
Serve warm alongside sliced hard-boiled eggs and pita bread.
tips: Ful is eaten every day in Egypt — the key is generous lemon and cumin. Tinned beans work perfectly; no need to soak.

name: Mansaf (Jordanian Lamb in Fermented Yogurt)
description: Jordan's ceremonial feast dish — large pieces of tender lamb slow-cooked in jameed (dried fermented goat yogurt), served on enormous platters of rice with a rich golden sauce.

ingredient_quantities.jameed broth: 2 cups (reconstituted from dried jameed, or sub full-fat yogurt)

instructions: Boil lamb with onion, whole spices, and salt until tender — about 90 minutes. Skim foam. Reserve broth.
Blend Greek yogurt with jameed broth until smooth; heat gently, stirring constantly (never boil or it splits).
Ladle 2 cups of the warm lamb broth into the yogurt sauce, stir, and keep on low.
Cook rice in remaining lamb broth with turmeric and butter.
To serve: mound rice on a huge platter, place lamb on top, ladle some yogurt sauce over everything.
Scatter almonds and pine nuts; garnish with parsley. Serve remaining sauce in a bowl alongside.
tips: Jameed (dried fermented yogurt) is the authentic base. Never boil the yogurt sauce — stir it constantly on the gentlest heat.

name: Maamoul (Date-Filled Shortbread Cookies)
description: Intricately moulded Levantine shortbread cookies filled with spiced dates or pistachios — the cookies of Eid, made in thousands by families who hand-press each one with carved wooden moulds.
instructions: Blend softened butter with semolina, flour, icing sugar, baking powder. Gradually add rose water and orange blossom water. Don't overwork — rest dough 30 minutes.
Process dates with cinnamon, cardamom, and 1 tbsp butter into a smooth, rollable paste.
Preheat oven to 170°C.
Take a walnut-sized piece of dough, hollow it, fill with a teaspoon of date paste. Seal and press into a maamoul mould (or shape by hand).
Bake 20–25 minutes until just barely golden — they should look almost underdone.
Cool completely; dust heavily with icing sugar before serving.
tips: Maamoul should be pale when done — they harden and crack if overbaked. They keep beautifully in tins for 2 weeks.

name: Luqaimat (Arabic Sweet Dumplings)
description: Pillowy, deep-fried dough balls drizzled with date syrup and dusted with sesame seeds — a beloved Emirati and Gulf street dessert eaten warm at the souq.
instructions: Dissolve yeast and sugar in warm water; rest 5 minutes until frothy.
Mix flour, salt, and cardamom. Pour in yeast mixture; stir into a smooth, thick batter.
Cover and let rise 1 hour until doubled and bubbly.
Heat oil to 170°C. Drop batter by small spoonfuls (or use two wet spoons to shape).
Fry 3–4 minutes, turning, until deep golden all over. Drain on paper towels.
Pile onto a plate, drizzle generously with warm date syrup, and sprinkle sesame seeds. Serve immediately.
tips: Wet your spoons with oil or water to prevent batter sticking. Fry in small batches to maintain oil temperature.
